case_name: State of New Hampshire v. Dominic Cherbonneau
transcript_text:
This is case 2024-0497, state of New Hampshire, versus Dominic Scherbenau.

Good morning, Attorney Witt. I understand you reserved two minutes.

Yes, Your Honor. Thank you.

And for the record, Justice Donovan is disqualified on this case. Please proceed.

Good morning. May it please the court. I'm Helen Witt on behalf of Dominic Scherbenau.

I'd like to address two points this morning.

First, the evidence that the state presented a trial was insufficient,

even under the exacting standard of taking all reasonable inferences in favor of the state,

to negate the reasonable inference that Scherbenau acted in self-defense.

The state did not prove that there was no reasonable inference inconsistent with guilt.

Second, Scherbenau's convictions and separate sentences on two felony riot charges

constituted double jeopardy. The evidence proved at most a single episode of violent

or tumultuous conduct, which is the unit of prosecution intended under the riot statute.

Finally, I'm also happy to take any questions and if time allows to address the issue of

the insufficiency of the evidence on the riot charge.

Can you explain how self-defense applies in a riot charge situation?

In this particular case, it's because the riot charge was premised with respect

to Mr. Scherbenau on his use of a firearm. So the charge and the enhancement

for one of the charges specifically related to his use of that gun

in connection with what the state alleged was the riot.

Would it apply to the bodily injury or injured person variant of the riot charge?

To the extent that the charges again against Mr. Scherbenau was related to his use of the gun

because he was not part of the group that was in the part of the parking lot

where there was pushing and shoving. He was removed from it in a different part of the parking lot.

Didn't the state essentially concede that he was not the culprit of the gunshot wound?

I guess the way I read the charge is that it was a riot because somebody received a gunshot wound

not because he caused it. Is that a fair reading?

Certainly he was not charged with causing the gunshot wound, the shot that ended up in the arm of the victim.

So let me just ask one more question. Would self-defense apply to that variant of the riot charge?

In this circumstance, yes, because the only reason that he could be charged

as part of the two or more people engaged in this violent tumultuous conduct

is because of the allegation that he shot the gun.

So you're saying that the shooting is sort of underlying the violent and tumultuous,

not necessarily just armed with a firearm?

Correct. With respect to him, certainly the state alleged that there were others

engaged in violent tumultuous conduct in the part of the parking lot where Mr. Scherbenow was not present.

Now with respect to the evaluation of the sufficiency of the evidence on the self-defense charge,

it's very important in the standard that this court applies to consider the entire record

and the evidentiary items in the record in this particular case

because of other things that exist in the record

and frankly because of things that don't exist in the record.

So the record with respect to self-defense is very limited

and there is no direct evidence of self-defense.

There were at least nine people in the parking lot that night

that the state alleged were part of what they thought constituted the riot,

but only one of those nine testified.

And that was Dante Torres who said nothing with respect to any of the issues that relate to self-defense.

The two victims, one who was the alleged target of the attempted first-degree assault,

did not testify even though the record makes clear that he was still in the parking lot when police arrived.

They knew who he was. He had a conversation with the state

and he apparently did not identify Mr. Scherbenow to police that night

because they had to go through other efforts to identify him.

CM, who was the victim who sustained the gunshot wound to the arm, also didn't testify

and was still in the parking lot when the police arrived that night.

There's nothing in the record that supports any inference that Scherbenow knew either of the victims prior to that night.

Wasn't CG the victim of the gunshot?

CG was the victim of the gunshot, yes.

I apologize if I misspoke.

And none of the physical evidence in the record bears on the self-defense claim at all.

The fact that there were evidence of gunshot residue in the cactus jacks and shells

doesn't bear at all on the issue of self-defense.

It wouldn't a combination of exhibit six, the video showing, at least as the trial court characterized it,

the defendant kind of moving forward and firing combined with the cactus jack strike.

Couldn't that rebut self-defense? Isn't that evidence that could rebut self-defense?

Only if other parts of exhibit six are ignored and or if the jury would assume some facts that weren't in evidence

because exhibit six also shows two people who the state believed

and the reasonable inference probably is that it was CM and CG

are standing in between Mr. Scherbenow and cactus jacks

and at the same moment that the shot goes through the window,

you see those figures stand up and the evidence also shows

and I don't believe this is contested because it was consistent with the state's witnesses' testimony at trial.

Those video shows those people doing what?

They're sort of standing up so there's a car in between, they're behind the car and they stand at virtually the same moment

and it's certainly the defense that at that point that's what led Mr. Scherbenow to have a reasonable belief

that because shots had already come from that direction, the state concedes that his shot was not the first shot

so he at that point had heard gunshots and people then standing towards him

is what led to his reasonable belief that unlawful force was likely to be coming in his direction at that point.

So it effectively, the question becomes without any direct evidence of the self-defense issue

the question effectively becomes whether exhibit six, the video, is sufficient to rule out every inference that is consistent with innocence.

What if the jury believed, I'm going to forget which witness the name of the friend who said back inside the Yi dynasty

the defendant was saying we got to go, let's go and is kind of running out of the restaurant after me

so wouldn't that combine, couldn't that combine with exhibit six and the cactus jack negates self-defense?

I mean he's running to the, if the jury were to believe that.

The jury would have a hard time believing it, it would not be a reasonable belief

even if they were to believe that single snippet of the testimony because of other direct evidence that contradicts it.

The videos of the doorway from the Yi dynasty that show people leaving

show that Sherbino and Torres didn't leave immediately after the man in the red tie-dye shirt

who the police believed was the aggressor. It shows them leaving close to I believe it's 30 seconds afterwards.

It's sort of a slow pace and the video inside the Yi dynasty shows Mr. Sherbino talking to some other people

and picking up his things before he leaves the Yi dynasty so it's not consistent with a suggestion

or an inference that he was leading a charge or trying to get everybody to run out quickly.

Given that the question really comes down to whether there's enough evidence, circumstantial evidence

from exhibit six, the video to negate any kind of issue, the context and some of the other factors

relating to that video also become very important. It's clearly the case that it's midnight

so it's very dark which makes the video very difficult to see.

Because there are multiple shooters as everybody concedes, it's very difficult to tell from any of the physical evidence

exactly who was shooting when or at what time.

Physical evidence showed multiple bullet holes in the car where CG and CM were standing

but it was impossible to figure out exactly which shot came first.

It does show that there were coming from multiple directions.

The theory that the police detective bombard had that the state testified to

was that there were probably multiple shots fired before the Shermano shot in different directions.

His belief which the jury didn't have to accept but it certainly is the case that it's a reasonable

inference if the state testimony was, it probably came from CM.

He probably shot the first shot and the man in the red tie-dye shirt shot back

so that those were the multiple shots going on in that part of the parking lot.

But the fact that it is uncontested that Shermano did not shoot first

and that the shots were coming from the direction in which he subsequently fired

certainly give rise to a reasonable inference that was not disproved that self-defense was appropriate.

There's nothing in the briefs that I saw but was there any evidence presented at trial as to why

Shermano would fire at CG and CM?

There was no evidence of that at all.

In fact, there was no evidence that there was any kind of prior relationship between Shermano and either of them.

There was no evidence of what any interaction might have been between him and either of them within the Yi.

The state suggested that there was enough evidence to raise an inference that there was bad blood

but the reality is there was no inference.

The cameras inside the Yi dynasty do show Mr. Shermano and others going in a direction

where the evidence suggested CM was within the Yi dynasty

but anything that took place among that group was not within the scope of the camera.

So the bottom line is that the totality of the evidence supported a reasonable inference of self-defense

that the state did not negate.

I'd just like to briefly address the trial court's conclusion that Mr. Shermano was not entitled to self-defense

because he could have retreated in complete safety.

There was really no clear pathway to leaving that parking lot with the gunshots at that point.

There were different cars in the way that posed risks of ricochet.

There were others at that point running with their backs towards where the shots that were coming from

including Shermano's friend Dante Torres

and Mr. Shermano simply had no obligation under the circumstances that existed at that point

to make himself a target by trying to run out of the parking lot.

I'd like to turn then to the issue with respect to the double jeopardy charge

He was charged with two separate charges of riot, both felonies.

One because he was armed with a deadly weapon and one because a person suffered personal injury.

But the evidence that the state presented with respect to the riot charges did not in fact prove multiple acts of riot

but rather only multiple factors in connection with riot

that elevated the crime of riot from a misdemeanor to a felony.

The unit of prosecution with which this court's cases have made clear is the appropriate analysis

when you're talking about this type of case for a riot is clear from the face of the statute.

The statute makes clear that a riot takes place when there are two or more people engaged simultaneously

in tumultuous or violent conduct that purposely or recklessly creates a substantial risk of causing public alarm.

So the gist or if you will, the unit of prosecution of the crime of riot

really comes down to engaging in tumultuous or violent conduct.

Tony, wait, you're at a late sign.

Thank you.

Thank you.

You reserved two minutes.

And Garland?

Thank you, Your Honor.

Please, the court.

My name is Sam Garland.

I represent the state of New Hampshire in this appeal.

You haven't heard any argument today, nor do I see an argument in the brief

that the defendant did not fire shots in the CVS parking lot.

And I just want to start there even though it's not in dispute

because I think it kind of helps capture what the evidentiary record looks like here, right?

We have this CVS video and it shows the defendant standing up, kind of walking around a car,

maybe even kind of running around the car and pointing in the direction of CM and CG

as the silver Hyundai facing toward him.

They dropped to the ground, simultaneous with that.

Detective Bombard testified that he linked up the timestamps in the videos.

You see kind of the debris coming into the cactus jacks window

and then you see that the officers ultimately identify a projectile hole there.

You have eight shell casings near where the defendant was standing.

You have Mr. LeJoy testifying.

He heard five to seven shots and then ran toward the scene

where he saw the defendant putting a gun into his belt and running away

and then finally Mr. LeJoy identified the car license plate

which resulted in the defendant being identified.

And so that's what the CVS video shows.

It's corroborated with both direct and circumstantial evidence.

And then if you take a step back and think about the self-defense argument,

what you have here is it really does start within the dynasty.

You have multiple witnesses who are testifying based on their observations that there was.

So is it the state's position that the riot started inside the restaurant?

No, I think the riot really for the purposes of this appeal is what happens

in the video in the CVS parking lot.

But for the purposes of the self-defense argument,

I think it is relevant kind of what happened in the Yi Dynasty

to lead up to going out into the parking lot

and there are multiple pieces of evidence that piece into that.

And so inside the Yi Dynasty you have multiple witnesses

who testify that they observed a skirmish.

There's no one who says that the defendant himself was part of that skirmish

but you do have this cell phone video,

Mr. Dombrowski's cell phone video that shows the defendant

and a few of his associates walking, I would say,

rapidly or aggressively toward where we know the skirmish was.

You have the testimony from Mr. Torres, which is,

Yo-Yo, let's get out of here.

That's what Mr. Chirpinot said and then they leave.

And there is a period of time.

It may be upwards of 30 seconds,

but it does link up with the CVS video at that point,

which is CM walking backward or he's initially walking forward

and then turns seemingly in response to a group of people following him

and starts walking backwards and is clearly having some sort of altercation

with people following him,

many of whom Detective Bombard identified as being the same people

the defendant was interacting with inside the Yi Dynasty.

That part's unclear to me.

So he's walking backward.

Is he walking backward from the rear entrance from Lake Yi?

So this was never established during trial

but there was testimony that he actually left through kind of a side fire entrance

and the owner of the Yi Dynasty who testified at trial

said that wasn't connected to any sort of alarm

that would go to the fire department.

So the understanding...

I think the inference is he left through the side entrance,

others left through kind of the front entrance

and then they started following him into the parking lot.

He notices they're there and you see in the video

he turns around and starts walking backward from the group.

That was with the defendant.

Is the side entrance towards CVS?

I believe it is, Your Honor,

but I'd have to go confirm that in the record.

I think it is.

But then you have in the video,

it's less than 30 seconds after

the defendant and Mr. Torres coming and joining that group

and so they're all kind of standing near the vehicles

that ultimately the defendant kind of hides in between

and the defendant joins that group.

He starts talking with the group.

You see him kind of pointing and seemingly gesturing toward the group

that then moves down toward the Silver Hyundai

and the defendant then kind of walks up around

and puts himself into a flanking position

near the dark vehicle

while they're kind of the remainder of the interaction,

the alteration happens down near the Silver Hyundai.

The defendant doesn't just stay there.

It's described as floating in the testimony,

but you can see in the video that he actually walks toward that group

as there's clearly some sort of argument going on,

people pointing at each other

and then kind of moves back and forth from there

in kind of that flanking position.

But then some sort of something happens

and I think it doesn't need to be established

beyond a reasonable doubt for the purposes of this appeal,

but the understanding is some shots were fired.

It wasn't the defendant at that point.

I shouldn't say everybody,

but most of the people flee from the scene at that point.

So you see people just kind of running out of the frame

in all sorts of different directions.

And the defendant himself at that point too

takes a few steps that look like he's going to flee

and then kind of resumes his position near the dark vehicle.

And it's 10 to 12 seconds by my count

between when everyone starts to run

and the defendant then steps up,

kind of walks around the vehicle,

starts opening fire on CM and CG.

Can you just address right there the stand your ground?

Yes, your honor.

And so from our perspective,

a reasonable jury could conclude beyond a reasonable doubt

that the defendant was initial aggressor in this

because he chose to affirmatively go into the parking lot.

He engaged with the group in the parking lot

before going over and flanking in that position

that he kind of, he re-engages with the group,

not directly with it,

but within a few feet of the group on the video.

Then when everybody else retreats under those circumstances,

he chooses to stay there.

And at that point, I think a reasonable jury

could conclude beyond a reasonable doubt

based on the combination of direct and circumstantial evidence

that he was an initial aggressor,

that everybody else was able to get out of the...

So your position is to clarify,

is that he could be an initial aggressor

even though he didn't fire the first shot?

I think that's right, or certainly that

there was no reason at that point for...

Yes, I think that is the answer,

but it could be because he was part of this kind of

ongoing interaction.

That started when he made the affirmative decision

and there's evidence to this effect

that he was going to go into this parking lot,

that he was going to be part of this interaction

and that he continued to kind of engage with that group.

And then he could have fled, and the video confirms that.

I think it confirms that certainly

beyond a reasonable doubt that everybody else

who ran was able to run out of the frame

before the defendant stands up.

Again, I think 10 to 12 seconds.

That doesn't sound long when I'm saying it to you here,

but it's a substantial period of time.

Everybody else in this kind of wide fish lens

shot is able to get out of the frame

and the defendant chooses to stay there,

then stand up and move toward CM and CG

and fire shots.

And under those circumstances, I do think

a reasonable jury taking the evidence

and the light most favorable to the state

could have concluded beyond a reasonable doubt

that he wasn't engaged in self-defense.

Is it visible on the video that he's actually firing shots?

It isn't visible...

No, so you don't see a firearm, your Honor,

you don't see kind of a flash of the shots.

What you have are, as him standing up,

you have him kind of holding his arm out

and I think that is visible, walking

or kind of running around one of the vehicles

and simultaneously CM and CG

dropping to the ground, which you can see

and you also have, you know,

again most of the evidence that I talked about

at the outset, you have the

Cactus Jacks video, the subsequent forensic

investigation of Cactus Jacks,

you have the shell casings

on the ground where he was standing,

you have the Mr. LaJoy saying

he saw him with a gun, Mr. LaJoy saying

he heard five to seven shots.

And I think all of that, some of which

is direct evidence of observations,

some of which is certainly

circumstantial and does require

an inference that he firelesses the shot,

but I can't think of any other reasonable

inference a jury could have drawn

from that cumulative evidence and I actually

I don't hear the defendant arguing otherwise.

And was CM facing

the defendant at the time

that you believe that the defendant

fired shots in his direction?

I'm not sure the video

is clear on that. So if

in the video CM and CG

are kind of in the upper right-hand quadrant near

the Silver Hyundai, it is darker

in that position than they're wearing darker clothing.

You see them both drop to the ground

at that point. And certainly

I think it's pretty conclusive the defendant was firing

in that direction based on all the evidence I just

said, but whether he had his back to the defendant

at that point, I'm not sure is clear from the video.

And there's certainly no testimony to that effect.

Is it also clear from the video

that CM

was engaged in

a firefight with

other people around the

Silver Hyundai?

So about 12 seconds before the shots,

something happens around the Silver Hyundai. I don't think

that's clear at all. I think the state's theory

during the trial was that CM

and maybe the person in the red tie-dye shirt were engaged

in some sort of firefight at that point.

I mean, there is other evidence to support

that he fired a weapon. He remained

on the scene, he provided his weapon the law

enforcement, he said it's been fired.

But you can't see on the video

exactly what happens then. You see everybody kind of

scram, like they run

out of the scene, but it's not

clear what exactly is happening at that exact moment.

But for the purposes of self-defense

I'm not sure that matters, right?

I do think you may be missing

clear evidence that

the defendant was under some sort of threat

that would have justified self-defense, and that probably

does help support that inference.

But in terms of what Justice Countway was asking,

which is the initial aggressor

and the defendant's ability to flee, I think

that's demonstrated by other evidence that's in the record.

That cumulatively supports that

inference beyond a reasonable doubt.

But the evidence also supports the reasonable

inference that CM

was firing at

the defendant before he

under this

hypothetical set of facts returned fire.

I'm not sure I would characterize it

as firing at and returning

fire, or that it supports that

inference. It supports the inference that

firearms went off at some

point in time, 10 to 12 seconds before

the defendant fired.

There are some bullet

evidence of

bullets hitting cars near where the defendant

is, but it's certainly not clear from the

video, nor is there any evidence that that was

targeting the defendant specifically.

In fact, there was some evidence that

the person in the tie-dye shirt who was part of that

were exchanging fire with each other.

In terms of the double jeopardy issue,

do you

think the trial court applied the correct

test or reasoning?

May I just

lay her onto that?

Could you just build in a response to

the defendant's argument on

State versus Belkner? Yes.

Absolutely. So starting

with that, if you'll

forgive me, Justice Countway.

I'll circle around.

I understand, and I read

that case in preparation for today, that it does

contemplate that

those are really enhancers of the riot.

But I don't think that case

forecloses the possibility that you could actually

engage in two different separate acts of

riot that are based on the enhancing

circumstances. And if you think of an example

of maybe something that feels more conventionally

like a riot, like a prison riot or something

like that, that the tumultuous

or violent conduct, particularly

tumultuous conduct, goes over a period of, let's say,

three or five minutes. If a person at

the beginning of that, who was engaged in it throughout

or came in and out of it,

like flashed a shiv or something like that,

he would be armed with a dangerous weapon, he could be charged

with riot, with felony riot.

If a person were injured,

regardless of whether it was because of the shiv, and the

person continued to be engaged in it, especially

if it were several minutes later, it could

be the same tumultuous conduct, but

two pretty clearly different

offenses in there. And I don't think

that the case that kind of talks about

and the Belcher case, I think

says that

that would be for double jeopardy purposes

inappropriate. I don't read

the decision to do that.

To your question, Justice Countway, I'm not

sure that the trial court applied the

correct standard, but I do think that the standard

that we have set forth on our brief

is the one that works under unit of prosecution

here, which is if the proof of

the elements of the charge would be

different, and they're basically not like

they wouldn't mutually prove one another

as indicted

that double jeopardy doesn't apply.

So if it's an aggravating factor

under Belchner, and I understand

your distinction, but

is it really a separate

element?

I think it is. And I was actually

here a couple of weeks ago talking about this case,

but the one that, so maybe this is why it came to mind.

But I think of it a little bit like

the factors in fortune for the venue

purposes, right? In that case, you have

the defendant who has to commit one specific act.

But this court described the remainder

of the things that have to happen there for

death resulting to be elements of that offense.

They operate like aggravating factors

or enhancements, but they really

are elements of the offense. And here to

have convictions of felony, you have to

have one or the other.

It's actually four different things with a disjunctive

in between that have to be proven. So I do view them as

elements. Even though it's in paragraph 4

as opposed to paragraph 1, which defines

the offense? I think so, Your Honor.

I didn't do and probably should have

in preparation for today find other examples

in the criminal code where it operates that way.

But I'm not aware of a case, I haven't

seen one cited that says that if

that sort of structure means it

isn't an element in order to prove

the offense. If you're right

on your prison riot analogy,

what are the two

events?

I mean, this is a relatively short

kind of

and the evidence is patchy.

But what are the two identify? I mean

in your prison riot analogy, you identify

sort of two specific

events that you could charge. I think

the jury could have found a couple of different things.

I think one thing the jury could have found, which I

admit we didn't brief clearly, was that there was

both tumultuous

and violent conduct here. And again,

that's another disjunctive in the statute. It says

tumultuous or violent conduct. And I think

the jury could find that he was engaged in

tumultuous conduct and with the strong

inference that he had, a conclusive

firearm, kind of when he engaged with this group, that was

in a brawl-like situation, and then

engaged in violent conduct when he stood up and fired

the gun. So I think that disjunctive is important

because it contemplates it can be either or.

I think two, the jury could have concluded

and I know this is a closer

question for double jeopardy purposes, that he

engaged really in both when he did stand

up and fire the firearm. But I think the jury

could have reasonably concluded the first

based specifically on the language of the statute

which it was instructed on there, which

uses a disjunctive, not a subjunctive.

So you could be charged for tumultuous

and violent conduct?

I think you could. I think the word or there

would signal you can. That you could be charged

with riot if you're engaged in tumultuous conduct.

You could be charged with riot if you're engaged

in violent conduct, and you could be charged with both

if you're engaged with both.

That isn't what was charged here.

What was charged separately?

It wasn't charged separately, but it was

instructed, as I understand it, under the

language the statute used, which used that

disjunctive or. And we did point out

that there was no request for a bill of particulars

or something like that. That would have clarified

that question. If the court has no further

questions, we'd ask that you affirm the convictions.

Thank you very much. Attorney Garland.

Attorney White. Thank you. Just two quick

points. First, we certainly agree

that the video is unclear.

And there are lots of parts of the video

that gave rise to

state arguments with respect to

inferences, testimony

about little pieces of dust or flashes

or things that look like gunshots.

But to the extent that there are

inferences raised, it is

clear that there are inferences raised that

both support and negate

guilt with respect to

the self-defense charge, because

the parts that are unclear

all go to the key

events, which is the alleged shooting,

who was shooting when and where.

So that ambiguity

given the standards that

the state was required to meet here

required them to be able to

disprove the inferences

that favor self-defense.

Secondly, with respect to the

state's argument that the evidence

supported the claim that

Mr. Sherbino might have been the initial

aggressor, we have a complete

section in the brief supporting the argument

that the evidence was not even sufficient

to support

the argument that he engaged

into multiple or violent conduct at

all. And the factors in

that brief show that he didn't

engage with the scrum

or the group of people who were in the

parking lot area

where the pushing and shoving was taking place.

Council said this morning

that the video makes clear that at one point

he walked in that direction

but it's clear

from Appendix, page

54, that he comes into

the parking lot significantly

later than the time

when the altercation began

and most of the other actors

are at the other side.

Can you just with your time left respond to the state's

argument on the double jeopardy issue?

Yes. I don't believe

first of all it's clear that it was not

tumultuous or violent

as charged here that the two

cases were or the two charges were

based on identical language

in the statute and identical conduct

and the Beltner case

certainly makes clear that that section

four isn't

to be considered part of

the element and that those factors

that are the aggravating factors

to move it into a felony

are not germane to

the ultimate issue. So it's

very similar to the situation

for instance in fog

where the statute

governed. Thank you.

Thank you very much Council. The case is submitted.

Court to take recess.

segments.text: This is case 2024-0497, state of New Hampshire, versus Dominic Scherbenau.
Good morning, Attorney Witt. I understand you reserved two minutes.
Yes, Your Honor. Thank you.
And for the record, Justice Donovan is disqualified on this case. Please proceed.
Good morning. May it please the court. I'm Helen Witt on behalf of Dominic Scherbenau.
I'd like to address two points this morning.
First, the evidence that the state presented a trial was insufficient,
even under the exacting standard of taking all reasonable inferences in favor of the state,
to negate the reasonable inference that Scherbenau acted in self-defense.
The state did not prove that there was no reasonable inference inconsistent with guilt.
Second, Scherbenau's convictions and separate sentences on two felony riot charges
constituted double jeopardy. The evidence proved at most a single episode of violent
or tumultuous conduct, which is the unit of prosecution intended under the riot statute.
Finally, I'm also happy to take any questions and if time allows to address the issue of
the insufficiency of the evidence on the riot charge.
Can you explain how self-defense applies in a riot charge situation?
In this particular case, it's because the riot charge was premised with respect
to Mr. Scherbenau on his use of a firearm. So the charge and the enhancement
for one of the charges specifically related to his use of that gun
in connection with what the state alleged was the riot.
Would it apply to the bodily injury or injured person variant of the riot charge?
To the extent that the charges again against Mr. Scherbenau was related to his use of the gun
because he was not part of the group that was in the part of the parking lot
where there was pushing and shoving. He was removed from it in a different part of the parking lot.
Didn't the state essentially concede that he was not the culprit of the gunshot wound?
I guess the way I read the charge is that it was a riot because somebody received a gunshot wound
not because he caused it. Is that a fair reading?
Certainly he was not charged with causing the gunshot wound, the shot that ended up in the arm of the victim.

I'd just like to briefly address the trial court's conclusion that Mr. Shermano was not entitled to self-defense
because he could have retreated in complete safety.
There was really no clear pathway to leaving that parking lot with the gunshots at that point.
There were different cars in the way that posed risks of ricochet.
There were others at that point running with their backs towards where the shots that were coming from
including Shermano's friend Dante Torres
and Mr. Shermano simply had no obligation under the circumstances that existed at that point
to make himself a target by trying to run out of the parking lot.
I'd like to turn then to the issue with respect to the double jeopardy charge
He was charged with two separate charges of riot, both felonies.
One because he was armed with a deadly weapon and one because a person suffered personal injury.
But the evidence that the state presented with respect to the riot charges did not in fact prove multiple acts of riot
but rather only multiple factors in connection with riot
that elevated the crime of riot from a misdemeanor to a felony.
The unit of prosecution with which this court's cases have made clear is the appropriate analysis
when you're talking about this type of case for a riot is clear from the face of the statute.
The statute makes clear that a riot takes place when there are two or more people engaged simultaneously
in tumultuous or violent conduct that purposely or recklessly creates a substantial risk of causing public alarm.
So the gist or if you will, the unit of prosecution of the crime of riot
really comes down to engaging in tumultuous or violent conduct.
